en.extract:
The yellow-throated warbler (Setophaga dominica) is a small migratory songbird species in the New World warbler family (Parulidae) found in temperate North America.


Taxonomy
The yellow-throated warbler was formally described in 1766 by the Swedish naturalist Carl Linnaeus in the twelfth edition of his Systema Naturae under the binomial name Motacilla dominica. Linnaeus based his account on "Le Figuier cendré de S. Dominigue" that had been described and illustrated in 1760 by the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson in his multivolume Ornithology. Linnaeus specified the location as Jamaica and Dominica but this was restricted to Santo Domingo in the Dominican Republic by the American Ornithologists' Union in 1910. The yellow-throated warbler is now one of over 30 species placed in the genus Setophaga that was introduced by the English naturalist William Swainson in 1827. The genus name Setophaga combines the Ancient Greek σης/sēs, σητος/sētos meaning "moth" with -φαγος/-phagos meaning "-eating".
Three subspecies are currently accepted:

S. d. albilora (Ridgway, 1873) – breeds in central east USA, west of the Appalachian Mountains
S. d. dominica (Linnaeus, 1766) – breeds in east, southeast USA, east of the Appalachian Mountains
S. d. stoddardi (Sutton, 1951) – breeds in northwest Florida and south Alabama (southeast USA), south of the Appalachian Mountains

The differences between the subspecies are small, and some authors treat the species as monotypic. The most obvious variation is that S. d. dominica usually (but not always) has some yellow at the front end of the supercilium, while S. d. albilora always has an entirely white supercilium. The southern subspecies S. d. stoddardi has the same supercilium variation as S. d. dominica, but differs in a slightly slenderer bill, and is non-migratory rather than a short-distance migrant like the other two subspecies.
A similar bird from the Bahamas was formerly treated as a fourth subspecies, but is now treated as a separate species, the Bahama warbler Setophaga flavescens. It was separated on the basis of having a longer bill, and in having extensive yellow down the entire underparts to the legs, versus white on the lower breast and belly in yellow-throated warbler.
Grace's warbler S. graciae from the southern Rocky Mountains is also closely related and similar in appearance, but lacks the white patch on the neck side, being uniformly gray there.
Yellow-throated warblers will occasionally hybridize with northern parulas (Setophaga americana), resulting in a hybrid known as "Sutton's warbler". Sutton's warblers lack the black streaks bordering the breast indicative of yellow-throated warblers, and have a suffused greenish-yellow wash on their back, which is also not indicative of yellow-throated warblers.  The Sutton's warbler was first discovered in 1940 in West Virginia.


Description

The yellow-throated warbler is 13–14 cm (5.1–5.5 in) long, with a wingspan of 21 cm (8.3 in), and weighs 8.5–11.3 g (0.30–0.40 oz).
In summer, male yellow-throated warblers display gray upperparts and wings, with double white wing bars. Their throats are yellow, and the remainder of their underparts are white, and are streaked with black on the flanks. Their heads are strongly patterned in black and white, with a long supercilium. The remiges and rectrices are black.
The females, immatures and non-breeding males resemble washed-out versions of the summer males; in particular they have a less crisply defined strong head pattern. They also have less bright yellows, and dark gray feathers instead of black ones in the body plumage. Compared to many other New World warblers, sexual dimorphism is slight.
The males' songs are clear, descending whistles. The calls are high sees or sharp chips.


Distribution and habitat
These birds breed in southeastern North America, and their breeding ranges extend from southern Pennsylvania and northern Missouri, to the Gulf of Mexico. One subspecies, from northwest Florida, is resident all year round. The other populations of this species are migratory, wintering at the Gulf Coast, eastern Central America, and the Caribbean. Vagrant wintering birds are sometimes seen in northernmost South America. 
In the United States, its range differs from typical Setophaga warblers because they have a more expansive resident population in the South than other Setophaga warblers. Moreover, their breeding range is more southerly, and their wintering range more is northerly, than the other warblers in the genus. According to McKay et al., "[t]he near absence of the species from the lower Piedmont of the Carolinas and Georgia ... presents a puzzle."
The yellow-throated warbler is a woodland species with a preference for coniferous or swamp tree species, in which it preferably nests. 


Behavior and ecology


Food and feeding
They are insectivorous, but will include a considerable amount of berries and nectar in their diet outside the breeding season. Food is typically picked off tree branches directly, but flying insects may be caught in a brief hover. They have also been observed catching and eating anoles.


Breeding
These birds build cup-shaped nests which are built in trees, and are concealed amongst conifer needles or Spanish moss (Tillandsia usneoides). Their clutches consist of 3–5 (usually 4) eggs.


References


External links

"Yellow-throated warbler media". Internet Bird Collection.
Yellow-throated warbler photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)
Yellow-throated warbler species account at Neotropical Birds (Cornell Lab of Ornithology)
"Yellow-throated warbler bird sound". Florida Museum of Natural History.

The '''yellow-throated warbler''' ('''''Setophaga dominica''''') is a small [[bird migration|migratory]] [[songbird]] [[species]] in the [[New World warbler]] [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] (Parulidae)<ref name="cursonetal1994">{{cite book |author=Jon Curson |title=New World Warblers |author2=David Quinn |author3=David Beadle |publisher=[[Helm Identification Guides|Christopher Helm]] |year=1994 |isbn=0-7136-3932-6 |location=London |name-list-style=amp}}</ref> found in [[temperate]] [[North America]].

==Taxonomy==
The yellow-throated warbler was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1766 by the Swedish naturalist [[Carl Linnaeus]] in the [[12th edition of Systema Naturae|twelfth edition]] of his ''[[Systema Naturae]]'' under the [[binomial nomenclature|binomial name]] ''Motacilla dominica''.<ref>{{cite book | last=Linnaeus | first=Carl | author-link=Carl Linnaeus | year=1766 | title=Systema naturae: per regna tria natura, secundum classes, ordines, genera, species, cum characteribus, differentiis, synonymis, locis | edition=12th | volume=1, Part 1 | publisher=Laurentii Salvii  | location=Holmiae (Stockholm) | language=Latin | page=334 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/42946530 }}</ref><ref name=paynter>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1968 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=14 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | pages=24–25 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14481225 }}</ref> Linnaeus based his account on "Le Figuier cendré de S. Dominigue" that had been described and illustrated in 1760 by the French zoologist [[Mathurin Jacques Brisson]] in his multivolume ''Ornithology''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Brisson | first=Mathurin Jacques | author-link=Mathurin Jacques Brisson | year=1760 | title=Ornithologie, ou, Méthode Contenant la Division des Oiseaux en Ordres, Sections, Genres, Especes & leurs Variétés | volume=3 | language=French, Latin | page=520, plate 27 fig. 3 | location=Paris | publisher=Jean-Baptiste Bauche | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35953579 }} The two stars (**) at the start of the section indicates that Brisson based his description on the examination of a specimen.</ref> Linnaeus specified the [[type location (biology)|location]] as Jamaica and Dominica but this was restricted to [[Santo Domingo]] in the [[Dominican Republic]] by the [[American Ornithologists' Union]] in 1910.<ref name=paynter/><ref>{{ cite book | author=Committee on Classification and Nomenclature| year=1910 | title=Check-list of North American Birds | edition=3rd | location=New York | publisher=American Ornithologists' Union | page=315 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/13674654 }}</ref> The yellow-throated warbler is now one of over 30 species placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Setophaga]]'' that was introduced by the English naturalist [[William Swainson]] in 1827.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=February 2025 | title=New World warblers, mitrospingid tanagers | work=IOC World Bird List Version 15.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/warblers/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=7 September 2025 }}</ref> The genus name ''Setophaga'' combines the [[Ancient Greek]] σης/''sēs'', σητος/''sētos'' meaning "moth" with -φαγος/''-phagos'' meaning "-eating".<ref>{{ cite web | last=Jobling | first=James A. | title=Setophaga | work=The Key to Scientific Names | url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/key-to-scientific-names/search?q=Setophaga | publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology | access-date=7 September 2025 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are currently accepted:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''S. d. albilora'' ([[Robert Ridgway|Ridgway]], 1873) – breeds in central east USA, west of the [[Appalachian Mountains]]
* ''S. d. dominica'' ([[Carl Linnaeus|Linnaeus]], 1766) – breeds in east, southeast USA, east of the Appalachian Mountains
* ''S. d. stoddardi'' ([[George Miksch Sutton|Sutton]], 1951) – breeds in northwest Florida and south Alabama (southeast USA), south of the Appalachian Mountains
[[File:Yellow-throated Warbler - 40977053314.jpg|left|thumb|''S. d. albilora'' in [[Castlewood State Park]], Missouri; pure white supercilium]]
The differences between the subspecies are small, and some authors treat the species as monotypic.<ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book | last=Hoyo | first=Josep del | title=All the Birds of the World | publisher=Lynx edicions | publication-place=Barcelona | date=2020 | isbn=978-84-16728-37-4 | page=798}}</ref> The most obvious variation is that ''S. d. dominica'' usually (but not always) has some yellow at the front end of the supercilium, while ''S. d. albilora'' always has an entirely white supercilium.<ref name = cursonetal1994 /> The southern subspecies ''S. d. stoddardi'' has the same supercilium variation as ''S. d. dominica'', but differs in a slightly slenderer bill, and is non-migratory rather than a short-distance migrant like the other two subspecies.<ref name = cursonetal1994 />

A similar bird from the [[Bahamas]] was formerly treated as a fourth subspecies, but is now treated as a separate species, the [[Bahama warbler]] ''Setophaga flavescens''. It was separated on the basis of having a longer [[Beak|bill]], and in having extensive yellow down the entire underparts to the legs, versus white on the lower breast and belly in yellow-throated warbler.<ref name="Hoyo"/><ref>Chesser, R. Terry, Richard C. Banks, F. Keith Barker, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Irby J. Lovette, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, James D. Rising, Douglas F. Stotz, Kevin Winker. 2011. Fifty-second supplement to the American Ornithologists' Union Check-List of North American Birds. Auk 128(3):600-613.</ref>

[[Grace's warbler]] ''S. graciae'' from the southern Rocky Mountains is also closely related and similar in appearance, but lacks the white patch on the neck side, being uniformly gray there.<ref name = cursonetal1994 />

Yellow-throated warblers will occasionally hybridize with [[Northern parula|northern parulas]] (''Setophaga americana''), resulting in a [[hybrid (biology)|hybrid]] known as "Sutton's warbler".<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Anich |first1=Nicholas |last2=Swelstad |first2=Jack |last3=Pierce |first3=Brian |date=2012 |title=The First Report of Sutton's Warbler (Northern Parula × Yellow-throated Warbler) in Wisconsin |url=https://www.academia.edu/34257810 |journal=The Passenger Pigeon |volume=74 |pages=149–154 |number=2}}</ref> Sutton's warblers lack the black streaks bordering the breast indicative of yellow-throated warblers, and have a suffused greenish-yellow wash on their back, which is also not indicative of yellow-throated warblers.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Devore |first=Leland S. |title=Sutton's Warbler in West Virginia |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/nab/v029n04/p00817-p00817.pdf |access-date=27 December 2022}}</ref>  The Sutton's warbler was first discovered in 1940 in [[West Virginia]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Bent | first=Arthur Cleveland | author-link=Arthur Cleveland Bent | date=1953 | title=Life Histories of North American Wood Warblers | series=Bulletin of the United States National Museum, No. 203 | location=Washington, DC | publisher=Smithsonian Institution | page=4 | hdl=10088/10009 | url=http://hdl.handle.net/10088/10009 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Yellow throated Warbler joby.jpg|thumb|Upperside coloration]]
[[File:Yellow-throated Warbler on territory.jpg|thumb|right|Yellow-throated warbler in [[Estell Manor]], [[New Jersey]].]]
The yellow-throated warbler is {{cvt|13|-|14|cm|in}} long, with a wingspan of {{cvt|21|cm|in}}, and weighs {{cvt|8.5|-|11.3|g|oz}}.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Yellow-throated Warbler Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Yellow-throated_Warbler/id|access-date=2020-09-30|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref>

In summer, male yellow-throated warblers display gray upperparts and wings, with double white wing bars. Their throats are yellow, and the remainder of their underparts are white, and are streaked with black on the flanks. Their heads are strongly patterned in black and white, with a long [[supercilium]]. The [[remiges]] and [[rectrices]] are black.<ref name = cursonetal1994 /><ref name=NGSBirds>{{cite book|editor1-last=Scott |editor1-first=Shirley L. |year=1994 |title=Field Guide to the Birds of North America |publisher=The National Geographic Society |isbn=0-87044-692-4 |edition=2nd |pages=[https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetobird00nati_0/page/366 366–367] |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetobird00nati_0/page/366}}</ref>

The females, immatures and non-breeding males resemble washed-out versions of the summer males; in particular they have a less crisply defined strong head pattern. They also have less bright yellows, and dark gray feathers instead of black ones in the body plumage. Compared to many other [[New World warblers]], [[sexual dimorphism]] is slight.<ref name = cursonetal1994 />

The males' songs are clear, descending whistles. The calls are high ''see''s or sharp ''chip''s.<ref name = cursonetal1994 />

==Distribution and habitat==
These birds breed in southeastern [[North America]], and their breeding ranges extend from southern [[Pennsylvania]] and northern [[Missouri]], to the [[Gulf of Mexico]]. One [[subspecies]], from northwest [[Florida]], is resident all year round. The other populations of this species are [[bird migration|migratory]], wintering at the Gulf Coast, eastern [[Central America]], and the [[Caribbean]]. Vagrant wintering birds are sometimes seen in northernmost [[South America]].<ref name = cursonetal1994 /><ref>{{cite journal |author=Ralf Strewe |author2=Cristobal Navarro |name-list-style=amp |year=2004 |title=New and noteworthy records of birds from the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta region, north-eastern Colombia |journal=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]] |volume=124 |issue=1 |pages=38–51 |url=http://www.alpec.org/Articulo%20BBOC/Strewe%20&%20Navarro%202004%20BBOC%20124%20(1).pdf |access-date=2020-03-17 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20051231225615/http://www.alpec.org/Articulo%20BBOC/Strewe%20%26%20Navarro%202004%20BBOC%20124%20%281%29.pdf |archive-date=2005-12-31 }}</ref> 

In the United States, its range differs from typical ''Setophaga'' warblers because they have a more expansive resident population in the South than other ''Setophaga'' warblers. Moreover, their breeding range is more southerly, and their wintering range more is northerly, than the other warblers in the genus.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=McKay |first1=Bailey |last2=Hall |first2=George A. |date=2020 |title=Yellow-throated Warbler (Setophaga dominica), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/yetwar/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.yetwar.01|url-access=subscription }}</ref> According to McKay et al., "[t]he near absence of the species from the lower Piedmont of the Carolinas and Georgia ... presents a puzzle."<ref name=":0" />

The yellow-throated warbler is a woodland species with a preference for [[conifer]]ous or swamp tree species, in which it preferably nests. 

==Behavior and ecology==
===Food and feeding===
They are [[insectivorous]], but will include a considerable amount of [[berries]] and [[nectar]] in their diet outside the breeding season. Food is typically picked off tree branches directly, but flying [[insect]]s may be caught in a brief hover.<ref name="cursonetal1994" /><ref>{{cite journal |author=Storrs L. Olson |author-link=Storrs Olson |author2=Helen F. James |author3=Charles A. Meister |name-list-style=amp |year=1981 |title=Winter field notes and specimen weights of Cayman Island Birds |journal=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]] |volume=101 |issue=3 |pages=339–346 |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/40825100#page/117/mode/1up |hdl=10088/6535}}</ref> They have also been observed catching and eating [[anoles]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Akresh |first=Michael E. |last2=Lamonde |first2=Steven |last3=Stokes |first3=Lillian |last4=Kent |first4=Cody M. |last5=Kahoun |first5=Frank |last6=Storr |first6=Janet M. Clarke |date=2022-05-10 |title=A review of wood warbler (Parulidae) predation of vertebrates and descriptions of three new observations |url=https://jco.birdscaribbean.org/ |journal=Journal of Caribbean Ornithology |language=en |volume=35 |pages=29–39 |doi=10.55431/jco.2022.35.29-39 |issn=1544-4953|doi-access=free }}</ref>

===Breeding===
These birds build cup-shaped nests which are built in trees, and are concealed amongst conifer needles or [[Spanish moss]] (''Tillandsia usneoides''). Their [[clutch (eggs)|clutches]] consist of 3–5 (usually 4) eggs.<ref name="cursonetal1994" />
